ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general murtabak entry; no tuna-specific murtabak code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pancake or crepe stuffed with meat or fish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers stuffed pancakes with meat or fish filling; no tuna murtabak-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for murtabak or tuna murtabak as a clinical food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Tuna Murtabak is approximately 250 g, representing one whole folded flatbread piece as typically served at a hawker stall or restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Murtabak (fish) — nutrient composition estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD fish murtabak entry and USDA FoodData Central tuna (canned in water) and wheat flour dough components
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: IMR MyFCD contains a fish murtabak entry (murtabak ikan) which is the closest regional match. Tuna-specific values were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central canned tuna (NDB 15121) and adjusted for the dough-to-filling ratio typical of a tuna murtabak (approximately 55% dough, 30% tuna filling, 15% egg and aromatics). Energy and macros are weighted composites. Values represent a cooked, pan-fried product per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of flatbreads and stuffed pancakes — analogous estimate based on roti canai / paratha GI data (GI ~57–62) from the University of Sydney GI Database and published Southeast Asian flatbread studies
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tuna murtabak. The GI of 57 is estimated by analogy with roti canai / paratha (GI 57, medium category) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database, which shares the same wheat-flour dough base and pan-frying method. The protein and fat content from the tuna filling moderately suppresses postprandial glucose response relative to plain flatbread. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 57.0) / 100 ≈ 32.5, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 22.8 × 2.5 = 57.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~390 mg per 100 g; ~975 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit tuna murtabak to occasional consumption, as one serving provides approximately 975 mg sodium — nearly 40–50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and should request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~57) and high glycemic load per serving (~32.5) due to refined wheat flour dough
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume tuna murtabak in small portions and pair it with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response, as the refined wheat flour base produces a medium GI and a high glycemic load per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g), elevated sodium, and pan-frying oil absorption; partially offset by omega-3 fatty acids from tuna
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of tuna murtabak due to its moderate saturated fat and high sodium content, while noting that the tuna filling contributes beneficial omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that may partially offset cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tuna Murtabak is a savoury stuffed flatbread dish popular in Singapore and Malaysia, representing a fish-based variation of the classic murtabak. The dish is made by preparing a thin, pliable wheat flour dough that is stretched and folded around a filling of seasoned tuna (typically canned or fresh), beaten egg, diced onion, garlic, green chilli, and aromatic spices such as cumin, coriander, and turmeric. The assembled parcel is then pan-fried on a flat griddle with generous cooking oil until the exterior is golden-brown and crisp while the interior remains moist and savoury. The result is a multi-layered flatbread with a flaky, slightly chewy crust and a rich, spiced fish filling. Tuna murtabak is commonly served with a side of curry sauce, pickled onions, and cucumber slices. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein from the tuna and egg, significant carbohydrate from the wheat dough, and notable sodium from seasoning and oil. The tuna filling contributes omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are beneficial for cardiovascular health. Clinically, the dish carries a moderate glycemic index (~57) and a high glycemic load per standard serving (~32.5), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Its sodium content (~975 mg per serving) is a concern for those with hypertension. In Singapore, tuna murtabak is available at Indian-Muslim hawker stalls and restaurants, and is considered a halal dish. It is distinct from the sweet Indonesian martabak manis and is more closely related to the savoury Arab-influenced murtabak tradition found across the Malay Archipelago.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tuna murtabak?
What is the difference between murtabak and martabak?
What is the GI of murtabak?
Where to buy tuna murtabak in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact tuna murtabak entry was found in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD fish murtabak (murtabak ikan) entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central canned tuna and wheat flour dough components. GI is estimated by analogy with roti canai/paratha from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD fish murtabak source entry and is not reliably estimated from component analogy for this dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD source and cannot be reliably estimated. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD source. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for canned tuna and wheat flour dough and are approximate. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g per 100 g from frying oil; this is a conservative estimate. All -1 sentinel values reflect genuine absence of data in the cited approved sources and are not confirmed zeros. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on roti canai/paratha GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for tuna murtabak was found in any approved source. Glycemic load per serving = (57 × 57.0) / 100 ≈ 32.5, based on 250 g serving size and 22.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Food group retained as 'Fish and seafood' per input, reflecting the primary protein source; the dish is a mixed product but fish is the defining filling ingredient. Halal status is based on the typical preparation of murtabak at Indian-Muslim establishments in Singapore; individual restaurant compliance should be verified.
